items.prompt: What element has the symbol H?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 1, the lightest and most abundant element in the universe
Name means 'water-former' from Greek hydro + genes
Hydrogen fusion powers main-sequence stars including the Sun
Makes up ~75% of all baryonic mass; key component of water (H₂O)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol He?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 2, second lightest element and second most abundant in the observable universe
First discovered in the solar spectrum during an 1868 eclipse before being found on Earth — named for Helios, Greek sun god
Only element first identified extraterrestrially
A noble gas that becomes superfluid near absolute zero; the only element that cannot be solidified at standard pressure

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Li?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 3, the lightest metal and lightest solid element
Name from Greek lithos ('stone'), discovered in the mineral petalite in 1817
Widely used in rechargeable batteries and as a mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder
One of three elements produced in Big Bang nucleosynthesis (along with hydrogen and helium)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Be?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 4, a rare alkaline earth metal
Named after the mineral beryl; emeralds and aquamarines are beryl gemstones colored by trace impurities
Exceptionally transparent to X-rays, used in X-ray tube windows
Highly toxic — inhalation causes chronic beryllium disease (berylliosis)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol B?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 5, a metalloid essential to plant cell walls
Name from Arabic buraq and Persian burah, referring to borax, known since ancient times as a flux
Borosilicate glass (Pyrex) resists thermal shock due to boron content
The only non-metal in Group 13; never found free in nature

items.prompt: What element has the symbol C?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 6, the basis of all known life and organic chemistry
Name from Latin carbo ('coal' or 'charcoal')
Forms more compounds than any other element — over 10 million known
Allotropes include diamond, graphite, graphene, buckminsterfullerene (C₆₀), and carbon nanotubes
Carbon-14 dating (half-life ~5,730 years) is used to date organic materials up to ~50,000 years old

items.prompt: What element has the symbol N?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 7, makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere by volume
Name from Greek nitron + genes ('niter-forming'), referring to potassium nitrate (saltpeter)
The Haber-Bosch process (1913 Nobel Prize) converts atmospheric N₂ to ammonia, enabling synthetic fertilizer that feeds roughly half the world's population
Triple bond in N₂ is one of the strongest in chemistry (945 kJ/mol)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol O?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 8, the most abundant element in Earth's crust by mass (~46%)
Name from Greek oxy + genes ('acid-former') — Lavoisier incorrectly believed all acids contained oxygen
Makes up 21% of Earth's atmosphere; produced by photosynthesis
Discovered independently by Scheele (~1772) and Priestley (1774), but Lavoisier named it

items.prompt: What element has the symbol F?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 9, the most electronegative and most reactive of all elements
Name from Latin fluere ('to flow'), after the mineral fluorite used as a flux in smelting
Isolation killed or injured multiple chemists — Moissan finally succeeded in 1886 (1906 Nobel Prize)
Used in Teflon (PTFE), uranium enrichment (UF₆), and fluoride toothpaste

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ne?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 10, a noble gas that produces a distinctive reddish-orange glow in discharge tubes
Name from Greek neos ('new'); discovered by Ramsay and Travers in 1898
True neon signs glow only red-orange — other colors use different gases or phosphor coatings
The least reactive element; no known stable compounds

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Na?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 11; symbol Na from Latin natrium, itself from Egyptian natron (a natural mineral salt)
English name from medieval Latin sodanum, a headache remedy
First isolated by Humphry Davy in 1807 via electrolysis of sodium hydroxide
Reacts violently with water; sodium vapor lamps produce the yellow light used in street lighting

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Mg?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 12, a lightweight alkaline earth metal
Named after Magnesia, a district of Thessaly in Greece
Burns with an intense white light — used in early photographic flash powder and incendiary weapons
Essential to life: the central atom in chlorophyll and a cofactor in over 300 enzyme systems in humans

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Al?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 13, the most abundant metal in Earth's crust
Once more precious than gold — Napoleon III served honored guests on aluminum plates while others used gold
The Washington Monument (1884) was capped with aluminum as a display of wealth
The Hall-Héroult process (1886) made mass production possible, crashing the price

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Si?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 14, the second most abundant element in Earth's crust after oxygen
Name from Latin silex ('flint'); a metalloid semiconductor that underpins the electronics industry
Silicon Valley named for the material in transistors and integrated circuits
Not to be confused with silicone, a synthetic polymer; makes up most sand as silicon dioxide (SiO₂)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol P?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 15; name from Greek phosphoros ('light-bearer') because white phosphorus glows in the dark
Discovered by Hennig Brand in 1669 from urine — the first element with a known discoverer
White phosphorus is pyrophoric and highly toxic; red phosphorus is used on matchbox striking surfaces
Essential to life: backbone of DNA/RNA, component of ATP, and ~1% of human body mass

items.prompt: What element has the symbol S?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 16, known since antiquity as 'brimstone' in the Bible
One of few elements found in pure form in nature, often near volcanic vents and hot springs
Key to vulcanization of rubber (discovered by Charles Goodyear, 1839) and sulfuric acid — the world's most-produced industrial chemical
IUPAC standardized the spelling 'sulfur' in 1990; 'sulphur' remains common in British English

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cl?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 17, a yellow-green toxic gas with a pungent smell
Name from Greek chloros ('pale green'); first produced by Scheele in 1774
Revolutionized public health through water chlorination, virtually eliminating waterborne diseases like cholera and typhoid
Used as a chemical weapon in World War I (first large-scale use at Ypres, 1915)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ar?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 18, the third most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere (~0.93%)
Name from Greek argon ('idle' or 'lazy') because it is so unreactive
Discovered by Lord Rayleigh and William Ramsay in 1894 after noticing atmospheric nitrogen was denser than chemical nitrogen
Used in incandescent and fluorescent light bulbs as an inert fill gas; also in welding to shield the arc

items.prompt: What element has the symbol K?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 19; symbol K from Latin kalium, derived from Arabic al-qalyah ('plant ashes')
English name from 'potash' — ashes of burned plants soaked in a pot
First metal isolated by electrolysis (Humphry Davy, 1807), just days before sodium
Essential electrolyte; bananas are famously potassium-rich; K-40 isotope makes them slightly radioactive

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ca?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 20, the most abundant metal in the human body
Name from Latin calx ('lime'); isolated by Humphry Davy in 1808 via electrolysis
Fifth most abundant element in Earth's crust; forms limestone, marble, chalk, and coral
Essential for bones, teeth, muscle contraction, and nerve signaling

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Sc?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 21, the first transition metal; a rare earth element
Named after Scandinavia; discovered by Lars Fredrik Nilson in 1879 in Scandinavian minerals
Predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-boron' in 1869 — its discovery confirmed the periodic table's validity
Used in aluminum-scandium alloys for aerospace and high-end bicycle frames

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ti?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 22, named after the Titans of Greek mythology
Discovered by William Gregor in 1791 in Cornwall; independently found by Klaproth in 1795
Exceptional strength-to-weight ratio and corrosion resistance; used in jet engines, spacecraft, and medical implants
Titanium dioxide (TiO₂) is the world's most widely used white pigment — in paint, sunscreen, and food coloring

items.prompt: What element has the symbol V?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 23, named after Vanadis, an Old Norse name for the goddess Freyja
Discovered twice: first by del Río in 1801 (retracted after doubts), then by Sefström in 1831
Forms compounds in many vivid colors; vanadium steel is exceptionally strong — Henry Ford used it in the Model T
Found in some sea squirts and certain mushrooms (Amanita muscaria) which concentrate it from the environment

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 24, named from Greek chroma ('color') because its compounds are vividly colored
Discovered by Louis Nicolas Vauquelin in 1797; the red of rubies and green of emeralds both come from chromium traces
Chrome plating provides a brilliant mirror finish and corrosion resistance
Stainless steel requires at least 10.5% chromium content; hexavalent chromium (Cr VI) is a carcinogen (as in the Erin Brockovich case)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Mn?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 25; name from Latin magnes, referring to the Magnesia region (same root as magnesium, but different elements)
Essential to steelmaking: removes sulfur and oxygen impurities; ~90% of manganese production goes to steel
Manganese dioxide has been used since prehistoric cave paintings (Lascaux) as a black pigment
The most abundant metal on the ocean floor, found in polymetallic nodules

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Fe?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 26; symbol Fe from Latin ferrum
The most abundant element on Earth by total mass (core is ~85% iron) and the most used metal
Iron Age began ~1200 BCE; wrought iron, cast iron, and steel are all iron alloys
Hemoglobin uses iron to transport oxygen in blood; Earth's liquid iron core generates the magnetic field

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Co?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 27; name from German Kobold ('goblin'), because cobalt ores were toxic and troublesome to miners
Cobalt blue pigment has been used since antiquity in Chinese porcelain and Egyptian jewelry
Cobalt-60 is a major gamma ray source used in radiation therapy and food sterilization
Critical component of lithium-ion batteries; most cobalt is mined in the Democratic Republic of Congo

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ni?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 28; name from German Kupfernickel ('copper demon' or 'Old Nick's copper') — miners cursed the ore that looked like copper but yielded none
The US five-cent coin is 75% copper, only 25% nickel
Highly resistant to corrosion; used in stainless steel, superalloys for jet engines, and nickel plating
Nickel is ferromagnetic — one of only four elements magnetic at room temperature (with Fe, Co, Gd)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cu?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 29; symbol Cu from Latin cuprum, named after Cyprus where Romans mined it extensively
One of few metals with a natural color other than gray/silver — distinctive reddish-orange
First metal to be smelted (~5000 BCE) and first alloyed with tin to make bronze, launching the Bronze Age
The Statue of Liberty contains 80 tons of copper; its green patina is copper carbonate formed by oxidation

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Zn?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 30; name possibly from German Zinke ('prong' or 'tooth'), referring to the pointed crystals of zinc ore
Used to galvanize steel (zinc coating prevents rust) — accounts for over half of zinc consumption
Alloyed with copper to make brass, known since antiquity
Essential trace element for humans; zinc lozenges are widely used as a cold remedy; zinc oxide is the white in calamine lotion and sunblock

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ga?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 31, named after Gallia (Latin for France)
Melts at 29.76°C — will liquefy in your hand
Used in semiconductors (GaAs) and LEDs
Predicted by Mendeleev as "eka-aluminium" before its 1875 discovery

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ge?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 32, named after Germany
Predicted by Mendeleev as "eka-silicon" — confirmed his periodic law
Used in early transistors before silicon took over
Transparent to infrared light, used in night-vision optics

items.prompt: What element has the symbol As?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 33, name from Greek arsenikon (potent)
Infamous as "inheritance powder" — the poison of choice in Renaissance Europe
Napoleon's wallpaper may have contained arsenic-based green pigment
Used in GaAs semiconductors and pressure-treated lumber

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Se?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 34, named after Selene (Greek moon goddess)
Named as companion to tellurium (named for Earth)
Essential trace nutrient but toxic in excess
Used in photocopiers due to its photoconductivity

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Br?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 35, from Greek bromos (stench)
One of only two elements liquid at room temperature (with mercury)
Historically used in "bromide" sedatives — origin of the word meaning a dull remark
Used in flame retardants and photographic film (silver bromide)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Kr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 36, from Greek kryptos (hidden)
Superman's home planet Krypton is named after this element
Its spectral line once defined the standard meter (1960–1983)
A noble gas, produces brilliant white light in discharge tubes

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Rb?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 37, from Latin rubidus (deep red) for its spectral lines
One of the most electropositive elements — ignites spontaneously in air
Rubidium atomic clocks are used in GPS satellites
Discovered via spectroscopy by Bunsen and Kirchhoff in 1861

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Sr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 38, named after Strontian, a village in Scotland
Burns with a brilliant red flame — used in fireworks and road flares
Strontium-90 is a dangerous nuclear fallout product (mimics calcium in bones)
Used in ferrite magnets and CRT television glass

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Y?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 39, named after the village of Ytterby, Sweden
One of four elements named after Ytterby (with Tb, Er, Yb)
Used in YAG lasers and red phosphors in old color TVs
Often grouped with the rare earth elements despite being a transition metal

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Zr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 40, from Persian zargun (gold-colored)
Cubic zirconia (ZrO₂) is the well-known diamond simulant
Used in nuclear reactor fuel rod cladding due to low neutron absorption
Extremely corrosion-resistant — used in chemical processing equipment

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Nb?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 41, named after Niobe (daughter of Tantalus in Greek myth)
Formerly called columbium in America — IUPAC chose niobium in 1949
Superconducting magnets in MRI machines and particle accelerators use Nb alloys
Brazil produces ~90% of the world's niobium

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Mo?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 42, from Greek molybdos (lead) — its ore was confused with lead
Essential trace element in biology, used by nitrogenase enzymes
Molybdenum steel alloys are extremely hard and heat-resistant
The word is famously difficult to spell and pronounce

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Tc?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 43, from Greek tekhnetos (artificial)
First artificially produced element (1937) — does not occur naturally on Earth
Tc-99m is the most widely used medical radioisotope for diagnostic imaging
Its absence in nature confirmed a gap Mendeleev had predicted

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ru?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 44, named after Ruthenia (Latin for Russia)
One of the rarest platinum group metals
Used in electrical contacts and chip resistors
Added to platinum and palladium alloys to increase hardness

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Rh?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 45, from Greek rhodon (rose) for the color of its salts
The most expensive precious metal — frequently exceeds gold's price by 5–10x
Used in catalytic converters to reduce NOx emissions
Plates the reflective coating on high-end mirrors and searchlights

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pd?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 46, named after the asteroid Pallas (itself named for Athena)
Key component in catalytic converters alongside platinum and rhodium
Absorbs up to 900 times its own volume of hydrogen gas
Used in dentistry, jewelry (white gold alloys), and hydrogen purification

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ag?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 47, symbol Ag from Latin argentum (shiny/white)
Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element
Argentina is named after this element ("land of silver")
Used in photography (silver halide), electronics, and antimicrobial coatings

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cd?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 48, from Latin cadmia (calamine ore)
Cadmium yellow was a favorite pigment of Monet and Van Gogh
NiCd (nickel-cadmium) rechargeable batteries were ubiquitous before lithium-ion
Highly toxic — Itai-itai disease in Japan was caused by cadmium pollution

items.prompt: What element has the symbol In?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 49, named for its indigo spectral line
Indium tin oxide (ITO) coats virtually every touchscreen and LCD display
So soft it can be cut with a knife and leaves a mark like a pencil
Produces a high-pitched "tin cry" when bent

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Sn?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 50, symbol Sn from Latin stannum
One of the earliest metals used by humans — essential for bronze (copper + tin)
The "tin cry" is the audible creak when a bar is bent
Tin cans are actually steel coated with a thin layer of tin

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Sb?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 51, symbol Sb from Latin stibium
Ancient Egyptians used stibnite (Sb₂S₃) as kohl eyeliner
Used in lead-antimony alloys for bullets and type metal (printing)
One of few elements that expands on freezing, like water

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Te?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 52, from Latin tellus (Earth)
One of the rarest stable elements in Earth's crust
Ingestion causes garlic-like breath odor that persists for weeks
Used in CdTe solar panels and Blu-ray disc rewritable layers

items.prompt: What element has the symbol I?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 53, from Greek ioeides (violet-colored)
Essential for thyroid hormones — deficiency causes goiter
Iodized salt, introduced in the 1920s, eliminated endemic goiter in many countries
Sublimes directly from solid to a striking violet gas

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Xe?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 54, from Greek xenos (stranger/foreign)
First noble gas proven to form chemical compounds (XeF₂, 1962)
Used in ion propulsion engines for spacecraft (Dawn, Starlink)
Xenon headlamps produce the distinctive blue-white light in luxury cars

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cs?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 55, from Latin caesius (sky blue) for its spectral lines
The SI second is defined by cesium-133 atomic clock transitions (9,192,631,770 Hz)
Most electropositive naturally occurring element
Melts at 28.5°C — liquid at slightly above room temperature

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ba?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 56, from Greek barys (heavy)
Barium sulfate "milkshake" is drunk for X-ray GI tract imaging
Burns with an apple-green flame — used in green fireworks
Bologna Stone (barium sulfate) was the first known persistent phosphorescent material

items.prompt: What element has the symbol La?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 57, from Greek lanthanein (to lie hidden)
First element in the lanthanide series, which is named after it
Used in high-refractive-index camera and telescope lenses
Misch metal (La/Ce alloy) is the flint in cigarette lighters

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ce?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 58, named after the dwarf planet Ceres
Most abundant of all rare earth elements
Cerium oxide is the primary compound used to polish glass and mirrors
Self-cleaning ovens use CeO₂ catalytic coatings

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 59, from Greek prasios didymos (green twin)
Originally part of "didymium" before being separated from neodymium in 1885
Didymium glass (Pr + Nd) is used by glassblowers to filter sodium flare
Used in high-strength permanent magnets and aircraft engines

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Nd?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 60, from Greek neos didymos (new twin)
NdFeB magnets are the strongest permanent magnets commercially available
Used in headphones, hard drives, wind turbines, and electric vehicle motors
Neodymium glass is used in astronomical filters and laser crystals (Nd:YAG)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pm?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 61; named after Prometheus, the Titan who stole fire from the gods
Only radioactive element among the lanthanides; all isotopes are unstable
Last discovered of the rare earth elements (1945, from uranium fission products)
Fewer than 600 grams exist naturally in Earth's crust at any time

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Sm?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 62; named after the mineral samarskite
First element named after a person: Colonel Vassili Samarsky-Bykhovets, a Russian mine official
Samarium-cobalt magnets retain strength at high temperatures, used in military and aerospace applications
Used in cancer treatment (samarium-153) for radiation therapy targeting bone metastases

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Eu?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 63; named after the continent of Europe
Most reactive and softest of the lanthanide elements
Europium phosphors produce the red glow in Euro banknotes under UV light as an anti-counterfeiting measure
Also responsible for the red color in older CRT television screens

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Gd?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 64; named after Johan Gadolin, Finnish chemist who investigated rare earths
Widely used as a contrast agent in MRI scans due to its strong paramagnetic properties
Has the highest neutron absorption cross-section of any known element
One of only four elements that are ferromagnetic near room temperature (along with Fe, Co, Ni)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Tb?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 65; one of four elements named after Ytterby, a village in Sweden (with yttrium, erbium, ytterbium)
Used in green phosphors for fluorescent lamps and color TV tubes
Discovered by Carl Gustaf Mosander in 1843 from cerium oxide
Soft enough to be cut with a knife

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Dy?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 66; from Greek dysprositos meaning 'hard to get at'
Not isolated in pure form until the 1950s, nearly 70 years after its discovery in 1886
Critical component in permanent magnets for electric vehicle motors and wind turbines
Has one of the highest magnetic susceptibilities of any element

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ho?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 67; named after Holmia, the Latin name for Stockholm
Has the highest magnetic moment (10.6 muB) of any naturally occurring element
Used as a pole piece to create the strongest static magnetic fields
Discovered independently by Soret and Delafontaine (1878) and Per Teodor Cleve

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Er?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 68; one of four elements named after Ytterby, a village in Sweden (with yttrium, terbium, ytterbium)
Erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs) are essential to modern fiber-optic telecommunications
Produces a distinctive pink color in glass and ceramics
Discovered by Carl Gustaf Mosander in 1843

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Tm?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 69; named after Thule, the ancient Greek name for Scandinavia or the far north
Rarest of the stable lanthanides (second-rarest overall after radioactive promethium)
Used as a radiation source in portable X-ray devices
Discovered by Per Teodor Cleve in 1879

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Yb?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 70; one of four elements named after Ytterby, a village in Sweden (with yttrium, terbium, erbium)
Ytterbium atomic clocks are among the most precise timekeeping devices ever built
Used to improve the strength of stainless steel alloys
Discovered by Jean Charles Galissard de Marignac in 1878

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Lu?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 71; named after Lutetia, the Latin name for Paris
Hardest, densest, and highest-melting of all the lanthanides
Independently discovered in 1907 by Georges Urbain (France), Carl Auer von Welsbach (Austria), and Charles James (USA)
Lutetium-176 is used in radiometric dating of meteorites

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Hf?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 72; named after Hafnia, the Latin name for Copenhagen
Discovered in 1923 by Coster and Hevesy using X-ray spectroscopy at the Niels Bohr Institute
Always found alongside zirconium in nature; extremely difficult to separate the two
Used in nuclear reactor control rods due to its high neutron absorption

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ta?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 73; named after Tantalus from Greek mythology, cursed to stand in water he could never drink
Named for its inability to absorb acid, echoing Tantalus's eternal frustration
Essential in capacitors for smartphones and electronics; classified as a conflict mineral
Highly biocompatible and used in surgical implants and suture clips

items.prompt: What element has the symbol W?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 74; symbol W from German 'Wolfram'
Highest melting point of any element (3,422 C)
Used in incandescent light bulb filaments and armor-piercing ammunition
Name means 'heavy stone' in Swedish (tung sten)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Re?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 75; named after the Rhine river (Latin: Rhenus)
Last stable, naturally occurring element to be discovered (1925 by Noddack and Tacke)
One of the rarest elements in Earth's crust; among the most expensive metals
Used in superalloys for jet engine turbine blades that must withstand extreme heat

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Os?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 76; from Greek osme meaning 'smell' due to its pungent oxide
Densest naturally occurring element (22.59 g/cm3), about twice as dense as lead
Discovered alongside iridium in 1803 by Smithson Tennant in platinum ore residues
Used in fountain pen nib tips and electrical contacts requiring extreme hardness

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ir?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 77; from Greek iris meaning 'rainbow,' for the varied colors of its salts
An iridium-rich clay layer worldwide marks the asteroid impact that killed the dinosaurs (66 Mya)
Second-densest element after osmium; most corrosion-resistant metal known
Used to define the kilogram in the former platinum-iridium standard

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pt?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 78; from Spanish platina meaning 'little silver'
Spanish conquistadors in Colombia dismissed it as inferior silver and discarded it
Essential in catalytic converters for automobiles; also a key chemotherapy drug (cisplatin)
Rarer than gold; roughly 30 times less abundant in Earth's crust

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Au?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 79; symbol Au from Latin aurum, meaning 'shining dawn'
One of the few elements found in pure form in nature; does not tarnish or corrode
All the gold ever mined would fit in roughly three Olympic swimming pools
So malleable that one ounce can be beaten into a 300-square-foot sheet

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Hg?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 80; symbol Hg from Latin hydrargyrum meaning 'liquid silver'
Only metal that is liquid at standard room temperature and pressure
Named after the Roman messenger god Mercury, associated with speed and mobility
Used in thermometers for centuries; now largely phased out due to toxicity

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Tl?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 81; from Greek thallos meaning 'green shoot,' for its bright green spectral line
Discovered in 1861 by William Crookes using the then-new technique of flame spectroscopy
Notorious as a poison: odorless, tasteless, and once widely used in rat poison (banned 1972)
Thallium poisoning causes distinctive hair loss, aiding forensic detection

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pb?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 82; symbol Pb from Latin plumbum, source of the word 'plumbing'
Romans used lead pipes for water systems; lead poisoning may have contributed to Rome's decline
First smelted around 7000 BC, making it one of the earliest metals used by humans
Once added to gasoline and paint; now banned in most countries due to neurotoxicity

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Bi?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 83; long considered the heaviest stable element until found to be very slightly radioactive in 2003
Half-life of bismuth-209 is 2.01 x 10^19 years, over a billion times the age of the universe
Active ingredient in Pepto-Bismol (bismuth subsalicylate)
Forms striking iridescent, rainbow-colored hopper crystals when slowly cooled

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Po?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 84; named by Marie Curie after her native Poland, then under foreign partition
First element discovered solely through its radioactivity (1898)
Used to assassinate Russian dissident Alexander Litvinenko in London in 2006
Over 100 billion times more toxic than hydrogen cyanide by weight

items.prompt: What element has the symbol At?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 85; from Greek astatos meaning 'unstable'
Rarest naturally occurring element: less than one gram exists in Earth's crust at any time
Most stable isotope (astatine-210) has a half-life of only 8.1 hours
A halogen like iodine, but the least reactive and most metallic member of that group

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Rn?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 86; named after radium, from whose decay it is produced
Only naturally radioactive noble gas; colorless and odorless
Leading cause of lung cancer among nonsmokers in the United States
Originally called 'radium emanation'; later briefly named niton (Latin: 'shining')

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Fr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 87; named after France, where Marguerite Perey discovered it in 1939
Most unstable naturally occurring element; longest-lived isotope has a half-life of only 22 minutes
Last element discovered in nature rather than synthesized
Bulk francium has never been observed; at most about 30 grams exist in Earth's crust at once

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ra?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 88; from Latin radius meaning 'ray'
Discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie in 1898 from uranium ore pitchblende
Once mixed with zinc sulfide to make glow-in-the-dark watch dials; the 'Radium Girls' who painted them suffered fatal radiation poisoning
A million times more radioactive than uranium by weight

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ac?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 89; from Greek aktis meaning 'ray' or 'beam'
Gives its name to the actinide series of 15 elements (89-103)
Glows pale blue in the dark from ionizing the surrounding air
Discovered by Andre-Louis Debierne in 1899 from pitchblende residues

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Th?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 90; named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder
Three to four times more abundant than uranium in Earth's crust
Proposed as a safer alternative nuclear fuel; thorium reactors produce far less long-lived waste
Discovered by Jons Jacob Berzelius in 1828; radioactivity detected independently by Curie and Schmidt in 1898

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pa?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 91, name means 'parent of actinium' because it decays into actinium
One of the rarest and most expensive naturally occurring elements
Co-discovered independently by Kasimir Fajans and Oswald Helmuth Gohring (1913), then by Frederick Soddy and John Cranston (1917)
Highly radioactive and toxic, with almost no practical applications

items.prompt: What element has the symbol U?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 92, named after the planet Uranus
Key fuel for nuclear reactors and nuclear weapons (U-235 is the fissile isotope)
Discovered by Martin Heinrich Klaproth in 1789
Depleted uranium used in armor-piercing munitions and radiation shielding

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Np?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 93, named after the planet Neptune (next planet after Uranus, as Np follows U in the periodic table)
First synthetic transuranium element, produced by Edwin McMillan and Philip Abelson in 1940 at Berkeley
Found in trace amounts in uranium ores as a decay product
Used in neutron detection equipment

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Pu?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 94, named after the dwarf planet Pluto (continuing the Uranus-Neptune-Pluto planetary naming pattern)
Discovered by Glenn Seaborg, Edwin McMillan, and others at Berkeley in 1940-41
Pu-239 was the fissile material in the Fat Man bomb dropped on Nagasaki
Seaborg initially wanted the symbol 'Pu' as a joke (like 'P.U.') and it stuck

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Am?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 95, named after the Americas, paralleling europium's naming after Europe
Discovered by Glenn Seaborg's team at the University of Chicago in 1944 during the Manhattan Project
Most widely deployed transuranic element: Am-241 is the ionizing source in household smoke detectors
Seaborg first revealed its existence on a children's radio quiz show in 1945, before formal publication

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cm?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 96, named after Marie and Pierre Curie, pioneers of radioactivity research
Discovered by Glenn Seaborg, Ralph James, and Albert Ghiorso in 1944
Used as an alpha particle source in X-ray spectrometers on Mars rovers (Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity)
So radioactive it glows in the dark due to its own radiation exciting surrounding air molecules

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Bk?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 97, named after Berkeley, California, where it was synthesized at UC Berkeley in 1949
Discovered by Glenn Seaborg, Stanley Thompson, and Albert Ghiorso
One of the least-used elements; producing even tiny amounts requires months of irradiation in a nuclear reactor
Parallels the naming of its lanthanide analog terbium (named after Ytterby, Sweden)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cf?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 98, named after California and the University of California, Berkeley
Discovered by Glenn Seaborg's team in 1950
Cf-252 is a powerful neutron source used to start nuclear reactors, detect gold and silver ores, and scan luggage for explosives
One of the most expensive substances on Earth, costing roughly $27 million per gram

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Es?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 99, named after Albert Einstein
Discovered in the fallout debris of the first hydrogen bomb test (Ivy Mike) in 1952
Its discovery was initially classified as part of Cold War nuclear weapons secrecy
Has no practical applications outside of scientific research due to scarcity and rapid decay

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Fm?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 100, named after Enrico Fermi, who built the first nuclear reactor (Chicago Pile-1)
Discovered alongside einsteinium in the debris of the 1952 Ivy Mike hydrogen bomb test
The heaviest element that can be produced by neutron bombardment of lighter elements
Its discovery was kept secret until 1955 due to Cold War classification

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Md?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 101, named after Dmitri Mendeleev, creator of the periodic table
Discovered by Glenn Seaborg, Albert Ghiorso, and others at Berkeley in 1955
First element produced one atom at a time, using a cyclotron to bombard einsteinium
Naming it after a Russian scientist during the Cold War was considered a bold diplomatic gesture

items.prompt: What element has the symbol No?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 102, named after Alfred Nobel, inventor of dynamite and founder of the Nobel Prize
Its discovery was contested for decades between Swedish, American, and Soviet teams
The Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna, Russia, is generally credited with the confirmed discovery in 1966
One of the few elements whose naming survived despite disputed discovery claims

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Lr?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 103, named after Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron particle accelerator
Last of the actinide series, discovered at Berkeley in 1961
Lawrence's cyclotron was the key tool that enabled discovery of many transuranium elements
Its symbol was changed from Lw to Lr by IUPAC in 1997

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Rf?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 104, named after Ernest Rutherford, the father of nuclear physics
First transactinide element; its discovery was disputed between Soviet and American teams during the Cold War (the 'Transfermium Wars')
Rutherford discovered the atomic nucleus and the proton, and first split the atom

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Db?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 105, named after Dubna, Russia, home of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research
Another element caught up in the Transfermium Wars naming dispute between the US and USSR
Americans proposed 'hahnium' (after Otto Hahn), but IUPAC settled on dubnium in 1997

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Sg?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 106, named after Glenn T. Seaborg, who co-discovered ten transuranium elements
The only element ever named after a living person at the time of naming (1997), breaking an old IUPAC tradition
IUPAC initially objected, but reversed course after widespread scientific support
Seaborg won the 1951 Nobel Prize in Chemistry and advised ten US presidents on nuclear policy

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Bh?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 107, named after Niels Bohr, the Danish physicist who developed the Bohr model of the atom
First reliably synthesized at the GSI Helmholtz Centre in Darmstadt, Germany, in 1981
Bohr won the 1922 Nobel Prize and later helped refugees flee Nazi-occupied Denmark

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Hs?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 108, named after Hesse (Latin: Hassia), the German state where Darmstadt is located
Synthesized at GSI Darmstadt in 1984 by Peter Armbruster and Gottfried Munzenberg
The heaviest element whose chemical properties have been experimentally studied

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Mt?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 109, named after Lise Meitner, the Austrian-Swedish physicist who co-discovered nuclear fission
Meitner was famously overlooked for the 1944 Nobel Prize, which went solely to her collaborator Otto Hahn
Synthesized at GSI Darmstadt in 1982

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ds?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 110, named after Darmstadt, Germany, where GSI Helmholtz Centre is located
Synthesized at GSI in 1994 by Peter Armbruster and Gottfried Munzenberg's team
GSI Darmstadt is responsible for discovering six elements (107-112)

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Rg?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 111, named after Wilhelm Rontgen, who discovered X-rays in 1895
Rontgen won the first-ever Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901
Synthesized at GSI Darmstadt in 1994

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Cn?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 112, named after Nicolaus Copernicus, who proposed the heliocentric model of the solar system
Synthesized at GSI Darmstadt in 1996
Officially named in 2010 on the 537th anniversary of Copernicus's birth

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Nh?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 113, named after Nihon, the Japanese word for Japan
First element discovered in Asia, synthesized at RIKEN in Wako, Japan, by Kosuke Morita's team
Officially named in 2016 after a decade-long priority dispute with Russian and American teams

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Fl?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 114, named after the Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions and its founder Georgy Flyorov
Flyorov famously wrote a letter to Stalin in 1942 urging the USSR to develop nuclear weapons
Synthesized at Dubna, Russia, in 1999 in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Mc?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 115, named after Moscow Oblast, where the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna is located
Synthesized in 2003 at Dubna in a Russian-American collaboration
Officially named in 2016 as part of the completion of the seventh row of the periodic table

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Lv?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 116, named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in Livermore, California
Synthesized in 2000 at Dubna in a Russian-American collaboration with Livermore
The lab was itself named after Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Ts?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 117, named after Tennessee, home of Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Oak Ridge produced the berkelium-249 target needed for its synthesis at Dubna in 2010
The second-heaviest halogen, though as a synthetic superheavy element its chemistry is largely theoretical

items.prompt: What element has the symbol Og?
items.explanation:
Atomic number 118, named after Yuri Oganessian, the Russian-Armenian nuclear physicist who led the discovery of multiple superheavy elements
The second person to have an element named after them while still alive (after Seaborg)
The heaviest known element and the last in the seventh period of the periodic table
A noble gas by position, but predicted to be a reactive solid due to relativistic effects on its electrons
Atomic number 2. The lightest noble gas, used in balloons and cryogenics.
Atomic number 10. Famous for its reddish-orange glow in neon signs.
Atomic number 18. Makes up ~0.93% of Earth's atmosphere, most abundant noble gas.
Atomic number 36. Used in high-performance lighting and photography.
Atomic number 54. Used in flash lamps, arc lamps, and ion propulsion.
Atomic number 86. Radioactive gas, can accumulate in buildings from soil.
